name: Ciudad Encantada y Ventano del Diablo — circular desde Cuenca
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Serrania Alta traverse a landscape characterized by craggy mountains, steep reliefs, and unique limestone formations. This region, part of the wider Serranía de Cuenca mountain range, features well-maintained, winding roads that follow river valleys like the Guadiela, Escabas, and Júcar. Cyclists will find a network of routes through dense pine forests and dramatic gorges, offering varied terrain for exploration. The area's low population density ensures minimal car traffic, contributing to a peaceful cycling experience. Best road cycling routes around Serrania Alta The most popular road cycling route is Ciudad Encantada y Ventano del Diablo — circular desde Cuenca, a 39.5 miles (63.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 1 minute to complete. This moderate route passes by the unique geological formations of Ciudad Encantada and offers views of the Júcar River canyon. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is San Antón Bridge – Júcar Gorge loop from Los Callejones de las Majadas, a difficult 62.6 miles (100.7 km) path. This challenging route features significant elevation gain and traverses the dramatic Júcar Gorge and the curious passages of Los Callejones de las Majadas. Local road cyclists also love the Trinidad Bridge – Hoz del Júcar loop from Cuenca, a 25.1 miles (40.4 km) trail leading through the Hoz del Júcar river landscape, often completed in about 2 hours 1 minute. Road cycling around Serrania Alta is defined by craggy mountains, dense pine forests, and dramatic river gorges. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with significant elevation gains. The routes in Serrania Alta are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 1,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Serrania Alta's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Serranía Alta?
A: Komoot offers over 60 road cycling routes in Serranía Alta, catering to various skill levels. You'll find a mix of moderate and challenging rides, with 26 moderate and 34 difficult routes, plus a few easier options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Serranía Alta?
A: Serranía Alta's routes are characterized by craggy mountains, steep reliefs, and unique limestone formations. You'll cycle on well-maintained, winding roads that often follow river valleys like the Guadiela, Escabas, and Júcar, passing through dense pine forests and dramatic gorges. Expect varied terrain with significant elevation gains on many routes.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Serranía Alta is known for its challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38157674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Antón Bridge – Júcar Gorge loop from Los Callejones de las Majadas&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 62.6-mile (100.7 km) path with significant elevation gain, traversing the dramatic Júcar Gorge. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38157348" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Callejones de Las Majadas – Hoz del Júcar loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt;, covering 81.2 km with over 1000 meters of climbing.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by unique natural attractions?
A: Absolutely. Many routes offer access to the region's geological wonders. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1373957013/ciudad-encantada-y-ventano-del-diablo-circular-desde-cuenca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciudad Encantada y Ventano del Diablo — circular desde Cuenca&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, passes by the unique rock formations of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5393773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciudad Encantada&lt;/a&gt; and offers views from the Mirador del Ventano del Diablo. You can also explore areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4396615" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Callejones of Las Majadas&lt;/a&gt;, known for its curious passages and monoliths.

Q: Are there any routes that feature waterfalls or river landscapes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in river landscapes and waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38173017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trinidad Bridge – Hoz del Júcar loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt; leads through the stunning Hoz del Júcar river landscape. While not directly on a road cycling route, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Cuervo River&lt;/a&gt; is a notable highlight nearby, famous for its travertine spring and impressive waterfalls, especially after rainfall.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Serranía Alta?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Serranía Alta, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists often praise the low population density, minimal car traffic, and the network of well-maintained, winding roads that traverse areas of great natural beauty, offering a peaceful and scenic riding experience.

Q: Are there any loop routes available for road cyclists?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Serranía Alta are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20137164" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Júcar Gorge – Palomera Village loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38173660" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuenca City Hall – Plaza Mayor of Cuenca loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt;, offering convenient circular journeys.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Serranía Alta?
A: The region offers enjoyable cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. While winter cycling is possible, be aware that higher elevations might experience colder temperatures and occasional snow, so checking road conditions is advisable.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for a moderate fitness level?
A: Yes, a significant portion of the routes, 26 out of 65, are classified as moderate. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40308846" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parador of Cuenca – San Antón Bridge loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 53.8 km route, offering a good balance of challenge and scenic beauty without being overly strenuous.

Q: Can I find routes that offer panoramic views?
A: Serranía Alta is abundant with panoramic viewpoints. The Mirador del Ventano del Diablo, accessible via routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1373957013/ciudad-encantada-y-ventano-del-diablo-circular-desde-cuenca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciudad Encantada y Ventano del Diablo — circular desde Cuenca&lt;/a&gt;, offers breathtaking views of the Júcar River canyon. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6268434" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrecho del Horcajo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is another excellent spot for expansive vistas.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that start from Cuenca?
A: Many routes conveniently start from Cuenca, making it a great base for exploration. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31870143" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoz de Huécar Viewpoint – Hoz del Júcar loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20136007" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Antón Bridge – Júcar Gorge loop from Cuenca&lt;/a&gt;, both offering diverse scenery directly from the city.
tips: The Hoz del Júcar in Cuenca is a spectacular place to explore by bike! 🚴‍♂️ The route through the Hoz del Júcar takes you through a stunning landscape, with the Júcar River accompanying you for much of the journey. The green waters of the river and the rock formations create a unique natural environment.🚵🌄

The Enchanted City, declared a Natural Site of National Interest in 1929. This world-famous geological phenomenon is made up of rock formations sculpted by the action of water, wind and ice, which, by eroding the rocks over centuries, have managed to shape more than curious figures (human silhouettes, objects and animals) with a precision that is difficult to understand, until achieving a city that seems to be asleep under some mysterious spell.

Mirador del Ventano del Diablo is a stunning natural balcony located just outside the town of Villalba de la Sierra, in the province of Cuenca. This viewpoint, carved into the rock, offers spectacular views of the canyon of the Júcar River, whose crystal-clear waters flow towards the city of Cuenca.
The name “Ventano del Diablo” comes from an ancient legend that says that the devil attracted visitors to this place, where they stumbled and fell due to the beauty of the landscape. From the viewpoint, you can observe the elegant flights of the griffon vultures and enjoy the lush vegetation surrounding the river.
It is a popular destination for nature and photography lovers, and is only 25 kilometers from Cuenca, making it an ideal stop if you are exploring the region.😎🚶‍♀️
